title: Evangelion and Love
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion and Love Post by ran1 » Tue Apr 27, 2010 4:03 pm In another thread, I used this image to represent what I believe to be the "love triangle" or "love trinity" whatever you want to call it. Essentially, if one of these parts of the triangles are broken, Love fails. Basically, I've given the characters in Eva some thought: they can all be condensed very easy into this triangle. Basically, what I mean to say is that their failures of the body, mind, and heart can all be seen here via this chart. Character Dissections: - Shinji : Fails to Love Himself, Triangle Broken *Cannot love others (Asuka, Misato) *His relationship with which gives him meaning is very flawed. - Misato : Fails to come to terms with the "Men" in her life, (Father, Kaiji, Shinji) "God" and "Other" broken *She realizes her inability to come to terms with herself as Shinji views her and Kaiji sleeping together - Asuka : Cannot come to terms with her status as a pilot, her "God" *Fails to love Shinji (her Other) *Ends up hating herself. That's all now, before I go into it further, I'd like some feedback. On Xard's request, I'll work in some of Kierkegaard's and Buber's (haven't read him ages ) theories, which should be enlightening, but for now... Thoughts? It's basically a compression of everything we talk about here so often, but I see it as a very effective one. EDIT: Requesting initial Xard reponse to be moved from the Misato the Slut thread so that my note on Kierkegaard and Buber makes sense.

[]: Post by Sailor Star Dust » Tue Apr 27, 2010 4:17 pm It's a blatant flame post that was already removed by OMF, so there's nothing to "move". Anyway, this looks like it'll be a good analysis. Do you plan on going into characters like Rei, Gendo, Kaworu(?), etc?

[]: Post by Xard » Tue Apr 27, 2010 4:17 pm Apparently it was a flame post (which I just...don't get. It was playful if anything) Anyway, as for Scruton: British philosopher Roger Scruton argues in An Intelligent Person’s Guide to Philosophy that sexual fantasizing is morally out of place because it devalues persons. Instead of deeming persons as objectively existing others, lust replaces persons with compliant images subject to one’s arbitrary mental manipulation. 'The fantasy blocks passage to reality.' The 'fantasy Other,' who is completely the instrument of one’s imagination, becomes merely an object to the one fantasizing. 'The sexual world of the fantasist is a world without subjects, in which others appear as objects only.' Scruton argues that the very mental act of such fantasies is an exercise in unhealthy and disrespectful unreality. One might call it psychic rape. (Scruton believes that if the fantasist becomes possessed by this image, rape is the natural result.)

[]: Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Apr 27, 2010 4:17 pm My opinion is that diagrams like this a simultaneously too simple and general. They are general enough to be applied to anything, yet too simple for much to be gained by using them. Essentially they are no real usefulness in discussion. You can accomplish as much with text, and in far less screen space.

[]: Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Apr 27, 2010 4:26 pm While the topic taken at face value seems like a plausible one for Discussion, I'm not sure how it's going to fly in the current febrile atmosphere of these forums. I also mourn the lost technique of ASCII art.

[]: Post by ran1 » Tue Apr 27, 2010 4:27 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: It's a blatant flame post that was already removed by OMF, so there's nothing to "move". Anyway, this looks like it'll be a good analysis. Do you plan on going into characters like Rei, Gendo, Kaworu(?), etc?

[]: Re: Evangelion and Love Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Tue Apr 27, 2010 4:30 pm ran1 : I quite like the way you have presented this, and find no flaw with your logic. However Asuka embraces her role as pilot, to an extent that she becomes unconsiously dependant. Otherwise this completely cuts to the heart of their romantic problems. Please don't quote whole posts, quote only the parts you're replying to.

[]: Post by planet news » Tue Apr 27, 2010 5:26 pm Self and other are definitely important. "God", on the other hand, can basically be anything and has already been shown in the examples you did to be far cries from actual god. Specifying what god is could make this "more relevant".

[]: Post by ran1 » Tue Apr 27, 2010 5:27 pm I suppose "God" would/should be replaced with "justification for existence".

[]: Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Apr 27, 2010 5:47 pm I just don't see any meaning for the entire "God" part of the pyramid. Asuka, for example, seems more like her problem is just getting along with anyone, period. Her Eva pilot status is certainly not the main problem like this model seems to insinuate.

[]: Post by ran1 » Tue Apr 27, 2010 5:58 pm I'm speaking not in real theological terms here, but people elevate things to the status of God, therefore, that is why I'm using God in quotations here. At no point did I ever feel that an actual ontological being ever factored into the minds of any of these characters. [Unless Yui counts as a transcendant metaphysical being]

[]: Post by NemZ » Tue Apr 27, 2010 6:27 pm I'm also completely failing to see what 'god' aspect of the theory adds. For example, Asuka's problem is lack of love for herself. If she did she wouldn't be so in need of proof of her worth and constant feedback from others. Piloting is her rationalized substitute for actual self-love. for another matter... is there anyone in the show who's problems truely fall on the other?

[]: Post by Legendary » Tue Apr 27, 2010 7:18 pm Let's see... Rei: Her lack of self-preservation causes all sorts of stuff. At the end of the day, her rejection of Gendo is empty because she simply elevates Shinji into his place. Ritsuko: She definitely has self-love issues, to the point of being willing to delude herself into thinking she's replaced Yui and her mother because that means that Gendo MUST love her. Gendo: See Rei, but with Yui as the elevated one, and without ANY personal growth. Hell, the only time I can think of a lack of self-respect NOT causing conflict is Toji and Shinji's issues in Episodes 3 and 4, and that's a pretty minor issue over the course of the series.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 27, 2010 10:41 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: My opinion is that diagrams like this a simultaneously too simple and general. They are general enough to be applied to anything, yet too simple for much to be gained by using them. Essentially they are no real usefulness in discussion. You can accomplish as much with text, and in far less screen space.

[]: Post by Legendary » Tue Apr 27, 2010 10:52 pm Seele seemed to love their God lots.

[]: Post by Allemann » Wed Apr 28, 2010 8:38 am Kierkegaard is rolling in his grave right now. Eva Yojimbo wrote: I'd object to the idea of "loving God" having any place in NGE.

[]: Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Apr 28, 2010 11:15 am When using the name of God (even in quotations), one must understand it's etymology . The filmmakers of Evangelion did put some thought into the names of characters and even the Angels before slapping them onto the figures we see in the television series. SPOILER: Show

[]: Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Apr 28, 2010 11:27 am ran1 wrote: I'm speaking not in real theological terms here, but people elevate things to the status of God, therefore, that is why I'm using God in quotations here. At no point did I ever feel that an actual ontological being ever factored into the minds of any of these characters. [Unless Yui counts as a transcendant metaphysical being]

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Apr 28, 2010 9:49 pm Allemann wrote: Objection overruled.
